question.text: What is a New Fund Offer (NFO) in mutual funds?

answer.text: The first-time subscription offer for a new mutual fund scheme launched by an AMC
A discount offer on existing mutual fund units
A rights issue by the mutual fund to existing investors
A bonus unit offer for loyal investors

explanation.text: **The Logic:** NFO (New Fund Offer) is the first-time offering of units of a new mutual fund scheme to the public. It is similar to an IPO in stocks. During the NFO period (usually 15 days for open-ended schemes), investors can subscribe to units typically at Rs. 10 per unit. After the NFO closes, units are allotted and the scheme opens for regular purchase and redemption. **The Trap:** NFO is not a discount offer or bonus. The Rs. 10 NAV during NFO does not mean the scheme is "cheaper" than an existing scheme with a higher NAV. A scheme with NAV of Rs. 100 and one with Rs. 10 can give the same percentage returns. **Cross-Exam Context:** Also tested in V-A with more details on NFO regulations. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Retired investors often get attracted to NFOs thinking Rs. 10 per unit is cheap. Explain that NAV is just a number and what matters is the percentage return, not the unit price. Existing schemes with a track record may be better choices.
